item.title: Admin Post - Disciplinary - Open
item.content:encoded:
friskynisky, if you believe an article should use a certain formatting element, contact the author directly via wikidot PM.

Give how many pages you can asked the same question on, this is an official warning. Please do not spam comments asking to add alternate formatting to pages.

item.title: Acs?
item.content:encoded: Can I add an ACS heading?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty spicy appendix A.
+1

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP mentions two "anomalous artists" (who I assume are not a part of AWCY?) who created this piece of work. I propose linking this to SCP-2015, considering that SCP-2015 mentions a gathering of anomalous artists from "around the world", and DREAmS could've been the creator of the one ridiculous platypus one.

Editing this:

Recovered documentation indicates that SCP-3028 was made in early 2011 by two individuals known only by their pseudonyms, "Hat Trick 6" and "DREAmS", both of which are active in the New York City anomalous art community.

To this:

Recovered documentation indicates that SCP-3028 was made in early 2011 by two individuals known only by their pseudonyms, "Hat Trick 6" and "DREAmS1", both of which are active in the New York City anomalous art community.

Footnotes
1. DREAmS is speculated to have also been involved in the creation of SCP-2015-█.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The Columbine shooters did nothing wrong."

OH NO

+1, creative and well-executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No role-playing 3028 in the comments, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nowhere in my post did I call you cishet or neurotypical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First of all, I'm neither cis nor neurotypical (nice assumptions here). And second, even tho I worded it in the way that is…not the best (guess I was sleepy, I get very dumb when sleep-deprived), I mostly objected towards such content as it's, essentially, fashionable, and a way to get cheap upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an enjoyable article; I liked that the joke was kept pretty succinct. Surprised this isn't tagged as an AWCY anomaly.

+ Some nitpicks:
- Still a +1 though

I agree with what some of the other folks said - the last half of the final addendum doesn't really cohere with the rest of the article. Mostly, I just don't understand the process that leads to researchers' actively requesting reassignment to SCP-3028 - why would they specifically select this (requesting reassignment to what is otherwise a pretty mundane anomaly, in-universe) as a method of self-harm? It seems inefficient, unless there's a memetic effect.

Also, the Foundation referring to stressing oneself out over irrelevant opinions as "self-harm" was just a little jarring. I'm also not 100% certain that the Foundation would reassign folks just because they'd chosen assignment to an anomaly that stresses them out, given how undeniably stressful a lot of the other anomalies are.

Anyway, good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get the addenda.

+ show block
– hide block

Are the people reading the articles and getting upset under an anomalous compulsion of some sort? Do the articles have a mild cognitohazardous effect causing the reader to believe that the articles are important?

When I get upset by an article posted online, I'm only upset because the person who created it genuinely believes it and is attempting to effect change that seems to me so obviously wrong. Now sometimes this comes from 'satire' sites that post false news articles in hopes of deceiving their targets and that angers me because someone is getting away with doing that, and is poisoning our ability to function as a society.

Here, the plant is just a plant. It doesn't think these things. It creates troll newspaper articles. If someone says "Chemotherapy is antifeminist" or "If Gandhi wanted to make a real change, he would have voted" I'd be upset at someone who so obviously missed the point. But if someone says that, doesn't believe it, and isn't trying to convince anyone that someone DOES believe that, then what's to be upset about?

People do tend to seek out things that make them angry, but those things make them angry for a REASON. They're angry that someone would try to do something like that. If YOU wrote a persuasive essay arguing that, say, LGBT people in homophobic environments should not attempt to leave them, that would upset me. My fiancee is stuck living with his abusive, homophobic grandmother.
And it would upset me that you'd want him to stay there.

But this plant is basically a particularly good bit of machine learning, like a highly advanced RoboRosewater. Why would anyone care?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article pokes fun at people who get themselves worked up over dumb internet politics. You seem to have gotten yourself worked up over how dumb and political you thought it was. Etiquette and policy aside, this is 10/10 for irony.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's some "tumblr bullshit" then: the site is full of people who are not cishet neurotypicals who write about characters and stories who are also not that. And thinking that an article critiquing people who go out of their way to get angry at news is "tumblr bullshit" says more about the reader.

EDIT: Also, your post is in violation of the Criticism Policy, specifically: "Critique the work, don't attack the writer" (because it's safe to assume that you mentioning more than one SCP of this nature means your "critique" is going after authors), "In short: Don't be an asshole.", and "Critiques should be helpful in some way" (this critique isn't helpful). I'd give it a read if I were you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I go to this wiki to escape tumblr bullshit, only to get face full of like 10 scips full of dumb memes and politic shit. Great ><

item.title: Nice Job!
item.content:encoded: I liked this SCP, and I really appreciate how you walked the fine line that let you make the statements truly absurd without actually strawmanning anyone. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of the article is good, but the final note just doesn't sit well with me for some reason. Neutral vote, but I would upvote if the final note was removed.
Edit: So, a lot of the time when people get mad about articles, they're getting mad about not the content of the article, but that someone genuinely believes these things and would post them online even though they are (from the reader's POV) obviously false. However, these articles don't come from anywhere. Nobody wrote them, so there isn't that hypothetical person who believes the information and thus nobody to get mad at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I echo the point about the final note, but I don't think it's enough to make it a bad article. On the whole it's really pretty good; if anything I think the commentary wasn't strong enough. The last note does some work in attempting to make up for the overall absence of it elsewhere in the article, but I think i would've liked to have seen more of that last bit mixed in elsewhere, as opposed to dumping it all at once.

Still, the image of a man intensely studying plants in order to fill a buzzfeed quota is more than a little amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the ending note isn't on the same level as the rest of the article, but it wasn't enough for me to think this SCP was ruined. I love the concept. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I enjoyed reading about this unique and interesting effect, and some details about its creation and further testing I also found enjoyable. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this and almost upvoted, but the random HMCL Supervisor include jarred me down to a no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never said it was false (setting aside any offshoot conversations about how the apparent demographics of the fictional Foundation compare to the real world).

It just broke the flow of the note for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume they're reading the full-length essays formed from -2, rather than just the initial statements

Whoops, I did misread that. (Embarrassing!) However, I'll still stand by my initial point; I can't see the researchers being affected by either the headlines or the full essay that strongly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you think that quoted section is false though?

Also I thought it was pretty clear that they were affected by the essays and not individual statements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite part was learning that anartists organized to raise money for charitable causes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea that professional adults are actually affected by 'Chemotherapy is anti-feminist' in any way beyond 'amusement' or 'dismissal'

I assume they're reading the full-length essays formed from -2, rather than just the initial statements. It's the difference between seeing a partisan/conspiracy clickbait headline or tweet, and actually clicking through to the article to take in all the vitriol and nonsense. It's still an absurd premise, but not as absurd.

I actually had just about the opposite reaction. I thought the anomaly and setup was a little pat and decent if long-winded, and that the "hot takes" mostly fell short of being fun-random. The final note was the high point of surprising and engaging personal narrative.

Still thinking on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creative core anomaly, and Trevor's plan of exploiting it to make money off of an Onion-esque site is charmingly clever both in- and out-of-universe. I really like the first half of this.

The final note kills any entertainment momentum you had built up over the course of the whole article, stopping the story dead in its tracks in order to deliver some nonsensical 'point'. The idea that professional adults are actually affected by 'Chemotherapy is anti-feminist' in any way beyond 'amusement' or 'dismissal' (to the point of requiring an expository note about this) is almost as ridiculous as SCP-3028-2 instances themselves.

Within the note itself, this line

for one, most researchers are already subject to bigoted statements that are both aimed at their particular demographic and popular among large segments of the population, so SCP-3028's provocations do not compare

is jarringly out-of-place as far as the content of the note is concerned, at least to me, and feels almost like it was thrown in as an afterthought to try to connect the skip more to readers.

Downvote for now, for pig-flesh-arm-endings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If anything, this hits a bit too close to home, especially given the modern political climate in the US. +1

item.title: "This should be the only SCP on the entire website."
item.content:encoded:
Biiiig thanks to Decibelles for reviewing this! I wanted to go for some sorta social commentary here (namely, 'there's a market for opinions that are meant primarily to be controversial, and the only real function of this content is to give people a way to stress themselves out), so, who knows what the reception will be.

The image is by Spencer Means; the original is here and under a CC BY-SA 2.0 license.

Thanks also goes to @nucleartakes on Twitter, for collecting posts that served as inspiration for a few of these.
